You know, it really comes to the approach of, you know, the only objections are in your mind, right? And the timidness of a salesperson. Zigg always said, timid salespeople have skinny kids, right? And so you really have to become professional. You really have to become a pro. We run it forward, focus on scripts, and think through all the objections and move through the sales process and become the best you can. And in the theater of mind, I always replayed on the way there a positive sales meeting. And on the way back, if it didn't go that well, because they all don't go that well, I don't have Dan's skill. But on the way back, I would play what happened, what went wrong, and it tests different things. So you say, I said this, I noticed their reaction, how do I adjust from those reactions and re-script myself. So, you know, it was on index cards at the time and I would go over and over and over them, because the penalty of failing was so high. I just couldn't afford to. I had burnt all the ships. And I really, really, it had to be successful. And that's how I approach their sales position after that. It has to be successful. And that's what you look for in your salespeople. And I think pros don't wing it. They don't leave it to chance. They don't let the little things get in their way. They really step forward. And that could come in your interview process for the salesperson. But sometimes when you're scaling, you can't catch everything. And when you get to 100, you're going to get some people. So you really want to take everything along the way and have a systematic process and a script that helps drive for success. Yeah. And just so if you're wondering, we keep talking about this bonus training that Nick and I are going to do. Since we're recording this live, obviously if you've bought this already and you're watching this, well, you've got the bonus CDs right in your package. If you haven't, when you invest in this system, we will be giving you a bonus training. It's actually going to be longer than like a 60-minute call. It's going to be a bonus training that we're going to do where we're going to be going over a mindset, where we're going to be going over a scripting, where we're going to be going over what to do to eliminate buyers or remorse, all the three things that Dan talked about. So again, if you've got the package in your hand and you're watching this, just go to your package and that's already in there. Okay. So there are a handful of objections that almost everybody gets. And I do want to go, we're going to do a little role-playing here with one of the bonuses that comes with this package. But let's talk about the core ones that almost everybody has and how we address them. All right? Now, we're not going to do an actual script here because the actual scripting is in this bonus report. But let's talk about price first. It costs too much. Well, so here's the most interesting thing about that. If you've done a competent job of value to price and specific relevance and so forth, then that objection is almost always a lie. It is really a mask over something that is either uncomfortable for them to express or that they really don't even know how to enunciate. So it is often a mask over, I can't afford it, right? Not that the price is too high. So hardly anybody, for example, would argue that the price for a Bentley, certainly after they've driven it, is too high. But there's a whole lot of people who cannot afford a $400,000 automobile. And there's a whole lot of people who would just feel really bad about buying a $400,000 automobile for themselves. So there's I can't afford it. I would feel horrible about spending that much money on an indulgence when there are starving people in Botswana, when there are stray cats with no homes, whatever. So this is often a way of them kind of fumbling around, right? Looking for help with something that they cannot enunciate or are not going to enunciate it. And people often read these things wrong in face-to-face selling, often in his case because they're in too much of a damn hurry. And because they're not scripted. So they are having to think about what they're going to say. So they can't even really stop and think about, well, what does this guy really mean, right? It is very memorable. And it was representative of a mistake that happens all the time when I was doing a lot of work with this hearing aid company. One of the sales interactions that we had taped and transcribed is at the point of the guy said, so this is like a $4,000 to $8,000 sale.
